other_observers: None; Original and sole observer of this bird.
specific_location: 501 Echo Lane (Pleasant Valley neighborhood), in my backyard.
description: Between pine siskin to house finch sized. Had a distinct red crown patch. Beak was short, conical, yellow. Dark, grayish patch on face around the beak. Eyes appeared black. Wings and tail feathers very similar to pine siskin or gold finch: dark and light streaked. Tail feathers appeared to spread out further than a pine siskin's. There was no indication of any eye patch or bar.  First impression was that I may have spotted some sort of sparrow (e.g., chipping or American Tree sparrow) or even a Cassin's finch. After comparing descriptions and pictures of such birds in my Stokes Western Region field guide and American Bird Conservancy field guide with my photographs, I noticed the redpoll (most likely the common variety) was an exact match for my bird despite being out of its normal range.  Several local birding experts (e.g., Ken Pals, John Drummo) came by and confirmed the identification of the bird in my photograph.Behaviors: Bird fed at my one of my nyjer thistle seed feeders and appeared with many pine siskins and a fewer number of house finch. Stokes field guide stated redpolls feed at thistle feeders.Call: Not heard.Plumage: Brown streaked, red crown patch
photographer: Photographer: David Perl, David Perl, David Perl
